sentences.text: --and maybe she'll come in and hold my hand.
[LAUGHTER]
--I understand.
Oh.
Correct.
Well, as we entered Auschwitz, there were people from every window looking at us.
They were prisoners there already from before.
Then there were the Russian prisoners.
They were-- I understand they were treated a little better.
And some of them even threw us some pieces of bread from the window, which was a pleasant surprise.
And--
I don't know how they became prisoners.
They must have-- must have been captured by the Germans.

What happened to them later on, I only know from the fact that in the camp that we were at-- I'm going a little bit ahead of myself-- in Gleiwitz, there were some Russian prisoners there with us also going to work every morning.
And one day-- what-- one of the-- one of the things the Germans did, and they did that well, every single day when, when, when you woke up in the morning, you had to stand outside.
For hours-- it seemed like for hours-- they were counting, counting, counting, counting.
When finally the tally was added up, then they reported to a higher-rank German that all is accounted for.
But this took-- they call it 'Appell.' This took hours of counting in freezing cold-- it could-- or very hot.
They just didn't care how long it took.
Anyway, one day, 11 Russians are missing.
And they found how they made their way out of there.
They were digging a tunnel underneath.
And anyway, they made their way out.
Well, two of them got caught.
No.
No, these were Russian non-Jews.
Yeah.
And two of those Russians got caught.
And they were hung out by their hand on, on a pole overnight, just, just tied up like that, stark naked, all night.
It was cold.
How they didn't freeze during the night, I don't know.
But the next morning, the entire camp, we didn't go to work.
The next morning, the entire camp had to be out.
And, and with big ceremonies, those two soldiers got hung.
It was the first time I ever saw, in real life, a hanging.
And I didn't want to see that, either.
I looked away.
And all of a sudden, I got a smack with a rubber hose that was yea thick, [POINTS] right on top of my hand.
You, [POINTS] look.
A German SS, he wanted me to look.
So I had to look right there as the hanging took place.
They, they died brave.
I mean, one of them said, long live Russia.
And the other one said something that I just didn't hear.
But I saw blood coming down his, his chin as his neck broke, you know.
I was just-- as hungry as I was, I didn't eat for, like, 24 hours.
I just couldn't digest the food.
It was a, a, a terrible thing for me to see as a young kid-- I mean, terrible thing to see at any time.
But that-- I, I have a, a lot of nightmares even now, not only about that, but also about my beating and some, some other things that will wake me up during the night.
Even my wife shakes me up sometimes.
You had a bad dream.
Wake up.
Oh, yeah.
But not as-- not as often.
Yes.
No.
It's--
Yes, more or less, a repetition.
Or something else comes up, you know, about, about the march that we did, and so on, the walking that we did for 21 days.
Well, one of them that comes back a number of times is during-- when the Russian guns were getting closer and closer, it sounded like next block.
One day, they make an announcement.
Like, it was, like, 2 o'clock in the morning.
Everybody out!
Take one blanket with you and nothing else.
And then we started marching.
We didn't know where we were going, maybe next day coming to a different camp.
But there was no next day.
Every single evening as it started to get dark, they herded us into a barn at a farm, where there were hay, for example.
And they herded in, like, 1,850 people, where there was room only for 400.
So people are sleeping standing up.
They were just exhausted, hungry.
And every morning, there was 30 or 40 dead people that you had to carry out.
And I just got lucky.
They never-- they never grabbed me to take-- maybe because I was short-- to take someone out.
[SIGHS] One-- I'm coming back now to Gleiwitz.
That was a regular, regular camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One night, I was grabbed to take out.
Right by the door, there was a barrel.
That's where you were supposed to go if you needed to do number one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when the barrel got full, they grabbed the next two people that came to take that barrel way out in the back of the-- of the camp and empty it.
And one time, I got unlucky.
And I got caught like that.
And without fear-- now, you see, I-- while you were afraid many times and all that-- without fear-- it was pitch dark-- I just squatted down.
And I crawled away.
Where did you go?
Where the hell did you go?
Who-- who-- they never-- he never found me.
He would have shot me right there, the, the older-- the barrack leader.
I don't mean shot me-- he didn't have a gun.
But he would have killed me.
But some other poor guy got caught the next, next-- to take out the barrel.
This is just a, a light side of the story.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.
It was full of liquid, you know?
And so of course it was heavy.
Yes.
It was very heavy.
I, I, I don't think any two people made it in one walk, you know.
They made it-- they may have been able to walk 10 paces and then stop, and then again 10 paces and stop.
It isn't-- the barrel was, was fixed so that you-- that you had, like, a rod that you put on your shoulder.
How should I say it?
There's a rod.
Here's the barrel in the middle.
And this end, you put on your shoulder.
And this end, the other guy put on his shoulder.
So you are carrying it on your shoulder.
Mm-hmm.
But while you were carrying it, you were making all kinds of mess at the same time, too, on yourself, and so on.
I only found that out from people that have been caught.
I never told anyone that this happened to me, not-- nobody, even until today.
[LAUGHS] But it just came back as a light side of the story because I beat them in this manner, you know.
But getting back to this walk--
Death march.
Well, it was later named the death march.
We didn't know it was a death march.
We started out, like, 1,850 prisoners.
And the Germans-- at every five paces or so on both sides, there was a German with a gun on his shoulder.
But at the rear end, there were, like, 10, 15 Germans that were closing up the rear end.
And--
Yes.
Yes, January, 1945.
Yeah.
Does this matter?
Or do I come back to some other thing?
Well, we started out in Gleiwitz.
The places that we arrived at every evening, as I said, was a barn.
I don't know the name of the towns.
One time, we went through a, a regular town.
And people were-- German people that lived there, they were standing on the side.
They were asking, who are these people?
You know, where they-- where did you come from?
They were asking the guards.
He says, they are [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] is like burglars.
In other words, we were criminals.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they, they took that as their answer.
But getting back to this-- where every morning, 30 or 40 people were dead.
We, we-- as we were marching for 21 days-- as it turns out, it totaled out 21 days.
During one period of time, we didn't have any drink or, or, or food for what-- drink-- water, we didn't have for about three days.
And everybody was dying of thirst.
And one time, we arrived at a body of water.
I want to say it wasn't a-- it wasn't a lake.
It was a little bigger than a pond-- a body of water.
And a lot of people ran towards that water.
And I had this-- it wasn't a classmate of mine, but somebody from my hometown.
And he jumped in and wanted to-- and Germans are holding you back with the machine gun.
Stand back!
Stand back!
That water is contaminated.
Most of them didn't understand.
They didn't speak German.
I understood what they're-- what they're yelling about.
And anyway, he made it to the water.
And he started drinking.
And one German said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
You want water?
Here, have some.
And he held his head down till he drowned.
This is-- comes back to me as a nightmare many times.
And another time-- this was a, a good friend from my hometown.
No, I was in the dream myself, yeah.
No.
Although one time, I must have been moaning because my wife woke me up and said, you had a bad dream.
And I immediately realized what the-- I, I knew what the dream-- what I was dreaming about.
Yeah.
Well, not every night.
[LAUGHS] But I, I, I have it.
It comes back, yeah.
No, I did tell it to-- then I have to go back to when I arrived to New York, you know.
That's another story in itself, which I will-- I will come to if you're interested.
OK.
I just want to finish this story because what happened is this was a bitter cold winter, a lot of snow-- knee-deep in many areas.
So it was difficult to march, to walk.
Yet the last row had it always the worst because you always heard a shot, and maybe 10 minutes later, another shot.
So later on, you found out, because pretty soon, the ones that were in last row, they ran forward.
And they wound up among the first rows-- first 5 or 10 rows, and so on.
And I did the same thing.
Pretty soon, I wind up pretty close to the last row again.
What happened, the Germans were having fun.
And every now and then, they were shooting down a guy that seemed very tired and all that.
But also, it happened that if you sat down for a moment because you couldn't take it, they shot you right there.
The snow was red with blood all the time, wherever you were walking.
And this friend of mine, who-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his father was, was the manager of a-- of the Jewish bank in Munkacs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he-- the two of us were walking always together.
The two of us did a lot of things together only because-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's another story.
I'll have to come back to that.
Anyway, he said to me, at, at one point, he says-- [NON-ENGLISH], I'm gonna sit--
OK.
So this friend of mine, he says to me, I can't take it anymore.
I'm going to sit down.
I said, no, you're not going to sit down because they're going to shoot you right there.
But I can't walk anymore.
I says, what am I gonna-- and I started yelling at him.
What am I going to tell your mother, you know, that I just let you die like that?
And you sat down, and they shot you?
You are not sitting down!
And I literally practically dragged him along-- I mean, talked with him.
And he finally made it to the barn that night, you know, and so on.
And so nothing happened.
And so he died two years ago in, in Tel Aviv.
I never-- I never did see him.
I was looking.
I was looking for him when I was in Israel.
And I just couldn't find where he was-- where he was living.
And one time, I heard about his address.
But he's not there.
He's having some kind of an operation.
He was sick.
And they, they, they might not have wanted me to see him because he was-- he didn't look too good, I understand.
He died of cancer.
At that time.
And I didn't know.
I just knew that, that I can't let him go.
What happened with, with him is two of my fellow hometowners, one of them-- two brothers-- the younger brother was working on a German kitchen.
And the older brother was working on the prisoner kitchen.
And he-- this, this guy that you say I saved his life, came to me.
He says, we have to talk to-- we have to talk to the two brothers.
I mean, their name was [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
We have to talk to [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
After all, we are from the same hometown.
They should help us and give us some extra food, you know.
And so finally, one day, I got up enough courage to get closer to the-- to the kitchen.
And psst!
You know, finally, he came out.
And I told him, you know, listen, you have a few people that you might consider friends.
And you should-- you should help us out with some food.
So one day, he said, all right, come, come to the back of the kitchen at 2 o'clock in the morning.
And we did that, the two of us.
We did that.
He brought out a big whole-- like a milk can full of boiled potatoes.
And I was eating boiled potatoes.
I stuffed it in my pockets and in my jacket.
I didn't know that that's like glue.
The next day, I was stiff.
I gave-- I gave away to some that I could trust that they're not going to tell on me where you got it, and so on.
This is another thing.
It had to remain a secret.
So there was a few friends of ours, like, like the one that had the 4343.
He was always in the clique, and so on.
And the reason why he was-- well, he was like a relative.
He was a brother-in-law to a cousin of mine.
Do you think those friendships helped you to survive?
Well, it was certainly a little extra food that I would not have had.
So it, it, it did help me in that respect not to get as thin as, as I did, or I would have gotten that much sooner.
Because later on, there was, like, an inspection every morning.
Some Germans came in.
And they started picking on people-- you, you, you.
And the ones that looked like they were going to die standing up-- and, and they just disappeared.
I never saw them-- never heard from them again.
For some reason, I, I was talking to myself-- I've got to survive this.
I've got to tell this to my relatives that are going to survive, and so on.
I just have to survive.
I kept pushing hope into myself.
And I, I guess it, it, it, it came out of me because I kept pushing hope into some of these other people that were surrounding me, like this friend that I'm talking about.
He passed away two years ago.
But anyway, this, this brother-in-law of, of my cousin, he was with me all the time in Gleiwitz.
Like I said, his number was 4343.
And we were working in a-- in a factory.
I think it belonged to Krupp.
They came-- what this factory had is boxcars, bombed-out boxcars, the iron twisted, the-- like the type of box cars that we were dragged in to Germany.
It, it had all kinds of damages from bombs.
And it was our job to straighten out what, what needed to be straightened out.
On the door, it was written in German-- in Hochdeutsch, in German-- what needs to be done to this wagon, to this boxcar, what needs to be done to that box car, and so on.
And pretty soon, [SIGHS] one Polish civilian that came in every morning from his home to work-- he worked for pay.
Six prisoners were assigned to this Polish prisoner-- to this Polish civilian.
Down at the next boxcar, another Polish private person, six other prisoners, and so on.
And he was assigning you every morning as we-- as we came in.
You, you do this.
You, you do that.
And one time, I was assigned to straighten out a-- an-- a piece of iron that was yea wide and long, I mean, very long.
And it was twisted.
So one guy had-- there was a-- there was a girl, a-- how do you call that when you-- when you cut iron in half with fire?
A welder.
Sawing it off?
A welder, you know.
It was a girl that had those big gloves.
And she was the welder.
She was heating up that piece of iron.
On one side, one guy had to stand there and hold something stiff, very strong.
And on the other side, a 16-pound iron-- you had to hammer it until the red iron got straightened out.
And I had to use that hammer.
It was heavy just to lift it.
And you do that for hours and hours.
And I just said, I'm not going to make it this way.
So next-- one morning, I came in.
And I started reading what's on the door, what needs to be done.
I said to my cousin-- I'm calling him my cousin.
It was my cousin's brother-in-law.
I says, let's do that.
He also read Hochdeutsch.
And we picked ourselves the work to be done before the Polish guy had a chance to put on his overalls.
You know, he just arrived to work.
And he's just getting his coffee.
And he's putting on overalls, and so on.
And then he starts reading what's wrong, what needs to be done, and then assigns you two, you do this.
You two, you do that.
He already saw the two of us are working.
So he left us alone.
And that's-- and that's the way I tried to do as many times as I could.
In Gleiwitz, yes.
And during that time, the girl that was the--
--the welder, she came with those big heavy gloves.
It was reaching up to here.
[POINTS] And one day, she goes, psst!
And she waves to me.
So I go over there.
And she gives me a cup of cream of wheat.
I, I, I thought I'd die right there from the surprise.
I never-- I never saw this in, in, in many years.
And then another time, she brought me half a sandwich.
She, she kind of took a liking to me.
One time, she said, I should go-- it was a boxcar, but it had a roof.
She told me to go inside and lay down.
She'll watch out.
She'll watch out for me.
And, and, and I did.
And then she, she came and gently woke me up, like, you'd better come out now.
And I remember one thing that comes back in my dream a few times.
One time, [SIGHS] at 5:00 or whenever, we started marching back to the camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the factory.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One guy's missing.
They're counting, counting, counting.
And one guy is missing.
And they finally found him.
He fell asleep.
And as he was running to be in the roster, they were hitting him and so on.
Anyway, to make the story short, as we were marching back to the camp, they shot him.
You know, they told him, go run over there.
Make your escape.
Nobody will look.
We'll, we'll let you, and so on, which was kind of foolish.
But even if he didn't do it, his, his fate was, was written there, you know?
And I remember when he-- they took a few guys to undress him, wash him, and so on.
And I, I saw a tiny hole in his body.
It was the first time I ever saw a person with a hole in his body.
It was traumatic.
I, I just couldn't forget that, just like the hanging.
I couldn't forget that for a long time.
Oh, yeah.
I still-- I still see it many times.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These were not pleasant days.
And I try to beat it out of my mind.
But for some reason or other, it comes back.
It never-- not one day passes by without me thinking of my parents, my brother, in one way or another.
And it just comes to mind.
Maybe for 10 seconds, but it comes to mind every single day, whatever I do.
Or night-- sometimes, I just wake up during the night to go to the bathroom.
I think of them.
No.
One, one scene that comes by, my younger brother, who was a handsome guy, he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to the well to bring some water into the kitchen.
And I was running after him, just sort of chasing him.
And he fell down.
And he cut himself right here in the middle, was bleeding like stuck pig.
And I ran physically, practically carrying him to the doctor, who was like-- you might say from here, it was, like, 10 blocks.
And he sewed him up and so on.
That, that-- somehow, that stands in my mind.
He still had-- he had a mark here for, for life.
So I felt like I was a bad boy.
But I-- but I wasn't, you know.
We were chasing each other many times for other reasons.
And this particular time, he slipped.
And he hit himself on the handle of the well.
So a lot of these things come to mind.
In Auschwitz, I was three weeks.
Nothing.
We did absolutely nothing.
We just-- we just wasted away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and, and we talked about food all the time--
--just food.
We were constantly hungry, all the time hungry.
And that's all we talked about.
OK.
